author: @reypolice5231
text:
I saw a report years ago that a NASA Engineer put a flexible Fernel lens (@50 USD) over the solar panel in a upside down ""U"" configuration, and got 8 times the output from the panel.

This was in space and heat was mitigated by the cold of space.

But he also demonstrated it on earth. So on earth, one may have to put water tubing at the back of the panel to cool the unit down.   This is then connected to heat a home and/or underground to dissipate the heat, with only the cost of an electric pump to move the water around.

So no tracking needed? Increased output 8 times ( estimate)
Cost of cooling equipment offset by using it to heat a home, shed, barn, greenhouse maybe.

author: @wibblywobblyidiotvision
text: Hello Robert.  Sun trackers are great fun to play with.  No need for microcontrollers or any of that jazz, passive (and passively powered) tracking controllers can be made quite easily, if you go back the "BEAM" robotics craze (well, fad, maybe) of the mid 90s.  A good search phrase might be "Wilf Rigter 2 axis head".

author: @jimbobarooney2861
text: The price of PV has plummeted over the last 20 years.  A 100W panel in 2005 was about 400 USD, now a 450W panel is approx 100 USD.  The framing is becoming as extensive as the panel's now.  Also in cloudy climates the tracking is not a hugh advantage, just simpler and much much cheaper to just add more panels, part of the beauty and simplicity if PV is there no mechanical parts

author: @n3qxc
text: Thoughts to ponder: Solar evacuated tubes to create pressure in a reservoir tank. This pressure would only be present on sunny days....this would be the energy used to move the panels with pneumatic cylinders. The cylinders could be controlled with cheap solenoid valves and a micro controller such as Arduino. Arduino could monitor the power output from the panel and be a closed loop system to move the panel. Less electrical use and less moving parts and hopefully less maintenance..... Your thoughts please?

author: @rickibaron4816
text: I like this idea.  I have a solar tracker that I bought for around $20.00 US.  I up my own solar array that has been running for over 8 years now.  The tracker I put on them have been working fine for years now.  My power output from the panels has gone up.  We are in the process of adding 5 kw more solar on our off grid garage and hope to tie it into our house as well.  We also have static panels on our roof, put up by a solar company and they do not produce as much power as they could if tracking was put on them.  So I think the cost is almost nothing.  We also have a airbase here in Tucson with many acres of solar panels on them.  There is a stretch of about 2 miles long that track the sun all day.  That seems to me that tracking is the way to go.

author: @salimufari
text: I think I prefer a worm drive or rack & pinion system. Depending on if you are tracking by elevation or rotation of the panels. Adjusting pitch through the support frame seems less stressful than doing a mid panel tipping system. Especially when accounting for wind flutter or deflection on the panel surface.

author: @Nikosi9
text:
Off topic, but I like the idea of a wind/water turbine using a toroidal propeller as the driver. Although you used a toroidal as an output fan on your air conditioner and cooling fan, I would request that you try to make a larger toroidal prop to mount on your turbine as an input driver, since it is more to scale. 
One of the main objections to neighborhood wind turbines is the noise they make. In many communities they are banned because of it. A toroidal "wheel" might be more acceptable than the clacking noise of the collapsible blades on your wind turbine. 
I have no skills and live in a tiny NYC apt, so...

author: @sinenomine9093
text: I really like your idea about using cycloid drives to align solar panels but I do want to just mention something observed with our fixed position  panels.  We live in Upstate NY with a fair share of cloud cover and less than optimum solar days.  Despite this, to my surprise, I have observed that particularly in late afternoon with the sun on the western horizon, if the sun can light the eastern & southern sky, the solar radiation reflected back on the panels can still generate significant power.  Obviously not as much as with direct radiation but still measurable.   Just a comment.

author: @uscitizen5656
text: Arduino or Pi with optical sensor so that it would track in real time. I've seen coding for a Pi but forgot about it. Building this as battery backup for my hydroponics system. Thank You!

author: @ChristieNel
text: My tuppence.  In the winter when the sun is below a certain angle your power goes effectively zero, so just tilting your panels south makes a massive difference.  For this scenario, I think tracking is worth it.  Depending on how you do the tilting, you can require a very high torque and produce huge stress when near the neutral position.

replies.author: @jimbobarooney2861
replies.text: It doesn't work that way. Solar irradiation in say the UK for December and January is very low. so you are adding a small percentage increase to a small amount to begin with, for alot of additional cost.  Simpler to set it fixed at a higher angle, or have part of the array set say 70 degrees from horizontal and the remainder at say 35 degrees, south facing

replies.author: @ChristieNel
replies.text: @jimbobarooney2861 It's not just a small percentage increase.  Imagine your panel is perpendicular to the sun and ignoring ambient light, then you'd get 0% of the sun.  Using trigonometry, if you tilt it just 30 degrees up, you'd get 50% of whatever is available.  Or more realistically, the sun falls down to 18 degrees in the winter.  Hitting an up pointing panel at this angle gives 31% of whatever gets through the atmosphere.  Tilting it 19 degrees to the sun, gives 18+19=37 degrees, and would double your power.

author: @401ksolar
text: Being a diy proponent I opted for an old C-Band satellite dish antenna actuator mount, it has a dc motor on the actuator arm that is moved manually a couple of times a day, angle point east all morning, straight south at lunchtime and angled west afternoon-evening, the elevation is moved once a week, sure it could be auto tracked but it's real simple to push a button a few times a day, and my chart for charge is way flatter than stationary did.

author: @hctim96

author: @mikejones-vd3fg
text: Yeah that was my conclusion with tracking too, takes a lot of gear. That extra 20-30% tracking gives you could be spent on more panel unless someone finds a more cheaper creative way, and this looks like it could help.  I found with my setup 3 positions one for morning one for afternoon peak and one for sunset us all I need to get about 95% what a trackback by system can give you so was thinking the of a simple timed switch, but still couldn't justify the work and setup and just left my panels alone this season and got a few more instead.  I'd be more interested in a mini Solar tracker that powers some land based drone bot that can climb trees and telephone poles for some nice shots.

replies.text: Don't know what you're referring to, but solar panels need to face the sun directly to generate at 100% of their ability. Any other tilt and you're losing efficiency.
